title: Inventory Search — Finding Items
description: Add search and sort capabilities to the inventory system. Store items in a string array. Write a searchItem() function that performs a linear search through the array to find an item by name, returning its index or -1 if not found. Write a sortInventory() function that sorts the items array alphabetically (using bubble sort or selection sort). Display the inventory before and after sorting. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
import re
assert 'searchItem' in cpp or 'search_item' in cpp or 'findItem' in cpp or 'find_item' in cpp, 'Create a search function (e.g., searchItem)'
sort_funcs = re.findall(r'void\s+sort\w*\s*\(', cpp)
assert len(sort_funcs) >= 1 or 'sort' in cpp.lower(), 'Create a sort function for the inventory'
assert 'string items' in cpp or 'string inventory' in cpp, 'Use a string array to store items'
assert '== target' in cpp or '== search' in cpp or '== item' in cpp or '== name' in cpp, 'Compare each array element to the search target'
assert 'return -1' in cpp or 'return(-1)' in cpp, 'Return -1 if the item is not found'
swap_pattern = re.search(r'(swap|temp|items\[.*\]\s*=)', cpp)
assert swap_pattern, 'Sort function should swap elements (use a temp variable or swap())'
acceptance_criteria: searchItem function performs a linear search through the items array
Search returns the index if found, -1 if not found
Sort uses bubble sort or selection sort with element swapping
Items are stored in a string array
Inventory can be displayed before and after sorting
hints: Linear search: loop from 0 to size, compare items[i] == target, return i if match
Bubble sort: nested loops, if (items[j] > items[j+1]) swap them
To swap strings: string temp = items[j]; items[j] = items[j+1]; items[j+1] = temp;
String comparison with > works alphabetically in C++ ("Banana" > "Apple" is true)
